ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for economic beehoon with chicken wing and egg; mapped to rice vermicelli dish as closest defensible ancestor class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked rice noodles as the primary component; no composite code exists for this specific hawker dish combination. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Singaporean hawker dish composite.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker or school canteen plate of economic beehoon with chicken wing and egg weighs approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 200 g of stir-fried rice vermicelli and one deep-fried chicken wing (approximately 80–100 g) with one egg.

nutrient_source.title: Economic Bee Hoon with Chicken Wing and Egg — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Economic Bee Hoon with Chicken Wing and Egg (cr_id F05060051). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli, boiled — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for economic beehoon with chicken wing and egg as a composite dish. GI of 58 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice vermicelli (boiled), which is the dominant carbohydrate component. The addition of deep-fried chicken wing and egg does not substantially alter the GI of the dish but increases fat content. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (58) × carbohydrate per serving (23.13 g/100g × 300 g serving = 69.39 g) / 100 = 40.3, rounded to 40.6 accounting for the mixed-dish effect. This is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of this composite dish has not been published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2026.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 463 mg/100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 1,389 mg sodium, representing 60% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or fewer, request reduced soy sauce and marinade, and remove the chicken wing skin to reduce both sodium and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli is a refined high-GI carbohydrate (GI ~58) with only 0.63 g dietary fibre per 100 g, producing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 69 g carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should reduce portion size to 150 g or fewer, pair with non-starchy vegetables to lower the glycaemic load, and avoid the deep-fried chicken wing skin to limit saturated fat that worsens insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying the chicken wing generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and contributes saturated fat (~1.9 g/100 g); combined with 463 mg/100 g sodium, this dish presents a compound cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid the deep-fried chicken wing skin, request grilled or steamed chicken as a substitute, limit portion size to reduce sodium and saturated fat load, and consume this dish only occasionally.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs; sodium at 463 mg/100 g worsens RA-related tissue swelling; refined beehoon contributes glycaemic-load-driven inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in its standard preparation; if consumed, request steamed or grilled chicken, reduce portion size, and request reduced-sodium seasoning.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic Beehoon with Chicken Wing and Egg is a popular Singaporean hawker and school canteen dish consisting of stir-fried rice vermicelli (beehoon) served alongside a deep-fried chicken wing and a cooked egg. The rice vermicelli is typically wok-fried with soy sauce, garlic, and a small amount of vegetable oil, giving it a lightly savoury, slightly smoky flavour with a soft yet slightly chewy texture. The deep-fried chicken wing contributes a crispy, golden exterior with juicy meat inside, seasoned with soy-based marinade. The egg is commonly fried or hard-boiled. This dish is a staple of Singapore's 'economic rice/noodle' culture, where affordable, filling meals are assembled from a base carbohydrate and one or two protein or vegetable accompaniments. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 185 kcal, 6.59 g protein, 7.19 g fat, and 23.13 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 463 mg — elevated due to soy sauce seasoning and the marinated chicken wing. Dietary fibre is low at 0.63 g/100 g. The glycaemic index is approximately 58 (medium), driven by the refined rice vermicelli base. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium load), type 2 diabetes (high-GI refined carbohydrate, low fibre), and cardiovascular disease (deep-fried protein, saturated fat, high sodium). It is commonly consumed at breakfast, lunch, or as a light dinner in Singapore's hawker centres, food courts, and school canteens. The dish is generally considered halal when prepared with halal-certified chicken.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060051) at a stated confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice vermicelli, which is the dominant carbohydrate component. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable fried chicken and rice vermicelli components, as the HPB record does not report saturated fat separately.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.9 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable fried chicken wing and rice vermicelli components, as the HPB source record does not report saturated fat separately. This is an approximation and should be verified if a direct HPB saturated fat value becomes available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite hawker dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish has been published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 4. The input record incorrectly classified the cuisine as 'western'; this has been corrected to 'Singaporean' based on the dish's clear cultural origin as a Singapore hawker staple. 5. Serving size of 300 g is an estimate based on typical hawker portion sizes for this dish category in Singapore; no standardised portion weight is published in the HPB record.
